problem: Forecasting dynamic behaviors of mammalian cell culture (viable cell density, mAb titer, metabolite concentrations) to enable proactive decision-making in bioprocess control for monoclonal antibody production.
method: Data-driven machine learning models combining multistep ahead forecasting strategies, feature selection, and AI algorithms (evaluated multiple combinations) trained on process analytical technology data from Chinese hamster ovary cell cultures.
finding: Identified optimal combinations of model elements balancing computational load with accuracy and reliability for predicting near-future culture performance, with systematic guidelines for bioprocess engineers to develop their own predictive models.
dataset: Chinese hamster ovary cell culture producing monoclonal antibodies
metric: Forecast accuracy for viable cell density, mAb titer, glucose, lactate, ammonia concentrations
baseline: Traditional bioprocess monitoring without predictive models
result: Successfully forecasted multistep ahead profiles enabling proactive culture management
enables: Enables implementation of interactive data-driven models within bioprocess digital twins for real-time optimization of therapeutic protein production.
limits: Limited to the specific cell culture system (CHO mAb-producing) and process conditions from which training data were collected; generalization to other cell lines or products requires new model development.
open_questions: How well do these models generalize to other mammalian cell lines and biopharmaceutical products beyond mAb production?

problem: Systematic gene knockout studies are prohibitive due to experimental resource constraints; need efficient virtual methods to investigate gene function across many genes using scRNA-seq data.
method: Virtual knockout tool that constructs gene regulatory networks from scRNA-seq wild-type data, virtually deletes target genes from the network, uses manifold alignment to identify differentially regulated genes, and infers target gene functions in specific cell types.
finding: scTenifoldKnk recapitulates findings from real animal knockout experiments and recovers expected gene functions in relevant cell types, validated against known knockout phenotypes.
dataset: Single-cell RNA sequencing data from wild-type samples
metric: Concordance with real animal knockout experiments; recovery of expected gene functions
baseline: Real animal knockout experiments; other computational knockout prediction methods
result: Successful virtual KO analysis matching experimental findings without performing wet-lab knockouts
enables: Enables systematic investigation of gene function across thousands of genes using only scRNA-seq data, reducing experimental burden and accelerating functional genomics research.
limits: Limited to gene regulatory network predictions from transcriptomic data alone; cannot capture post-translational modifications, protein-protein interactions not detectable from scRNA-seq, or tissue-level effects.
open_questions: How accurately does virtual knockout performance translate to cell types not well-represented in the training scRNA-seq data, and does it capture epistatic interactions between multiple gene knockouts?

problem: Understanding complex retinal disease and developing interventions requires integrating diverse datasets on outer retina structure and function into computational models of whole-cell and -tissue systems.
method: Review of integrative computational modeling approaches that map experimental data onto whole-cell and whole-tissue simulations of the human outer retina, integrating photoreceptor and retinal pigment epithelial cell physiology.
finding: Mapping diverse datasets onto integrative computational models represents a pivotal approach for understanding complex retinal disease; discusses both opportunities and challenges in implementing such models.
dataset: Outer retina tissues (photoreceptor cells, retinal pigment epithelial cells)
metric: Model fidelity and completeness in capturing retinal structure and function
baseline: Traditional experimental approaches studying retinal components in isolation
result: Conceptual framework and research agenda for whole-cell and -tissue retinal simulations
enables: Enables development of mechanistic models to predict retinal responses to disease and test novel interventions in silico before clinical application.
limits: Review addresses the challenges and gaps in obtaining sufficient experimental data on all aspects of retinal dynamics; significant data integration challenges remain for mammalian tissues.
open_questions: What are the critical minimal datasets needed to build predictive whole-cell models of the outer retina, and how can compartmentalization and cell-cell signaling be adequately represented?

problem: TRPML3 cellular expression on whole-tissue level remains largely elusive despite known roles in specific cell types; comprehensive mapping of TRPML3 expression across organs is needed.
method: Generated τGFP reporter mouse model for TRPML3 and compared expression patterns across organs and tissues using immunofluorescence, immunohistochemistry, and in situ hybridization techniques.
finding: Uncovered widespread TRPML3 expression in secretory cells and endocrine glands including alveolar macrophages, melanocytes, kidney principal cells, olfactory sensory neurons, and multiple endocrine glands (parathyroid, thyroid, salivary, adrenal, pituitary) plus reproductive organs.
dataset: Mouse tissues (whole-body organs and glands)
metric: Spatial localization and expression pattern mapping across cell types and tissues
baseline: Previous TRPML3 antibody studies and in situ hybridization data
result: Comprehensive tissue-level expression map revealing TRPML3 in secretory cells and endocrine glands; expression in alveolar macrophages confirmed
enables: Enables investigation of TRPML3 function in hormone secretion, nutrient uptake, and exosome-bacterial defense across diverse physiological systems.
limits: Limited to mouse tissues; cannot directly extrapolate findings to human TRPML3 expression patterns; reporter mouse model may show different expression than endogenous TRPML3 protein.
open_questions: What are the specific functional roles of TRPML3 in hormone secretion and endocrine function, and do human tissues show identical expression patterns to the mouse model?

problem: Discovery of novel antimicrobial and cytotoxic agents effective against colorectal cancer and bacterial pathogens for therapeutic applications.
method: Chemical synthesis of naphthol diazenyl scaffold-based Schiff bases via condensation reactions; in vitro evaluation of antimicrobial activity against bacteria/fungi and cytotoxic potential against HT-29 cancer cells; apoptosis and cell-cycle analysis via flow cytometry.
finding: Compounds NS-2, NS-8, NS-21, and NS-23 showed maximum cytotoxicity toward HT-29 cells with IC50 values 4-19 μg/ml, inducing >90% (NS-2) and 62% (NS-21) apoptosis, with cell-cycle arrest in S and G2/M phases comparable to doxorubicin.
dataset: E. coli, S. enterica, B. subtilis, A. fumigatus, HT-29 human colorectal carcinoma cells
metric: IC50 concentration; apoptosis percentage; cell-cycle arrest phase; comparison to doxorubicin
baseline: Doxorubicin (68% apoptosis); existing antimicrobial and cytotoxic agents
result: IC50 range 4-19 μg/ml; NS-2 and NS-21 induce 90% and 62% apoptosis respectively; cell-cycle arrest in S and G2/M phases
enables: Enables development of novel antimicrobial and anticancer therapeutic candidates with potential for colorectal cancer treatment and bacterial infection control.
limits: Limited to in vitro studies; no in vivo efficacy, pharmacokinetics, or toxicity data; limited structural diversity explored; unclear selectivity between cancer and normal cells.
open_questions: Do these compounds retain activity in in vivo tumor models, what are their pharmacokinetic properties and in vivo toxicity profiles, and do they selectively target cancer cells over healthy tissue?

problem: Predicting evolution of multicellular systems requires mathematical modeling that systematically integrates biological knowledge with multi-omics data; current approaches lack interpretability and scalability.
method: Proposes cell behavior hypothesis grammar using natural language cell rules to create mathematical models; demonstrates integration with multi-omics data to generate in silico models; reference implementation provided with examples in cancer and brain development.
finding: Demonstrates that natural language grammar can systematically convert biological hypotheses into executable mathematical models enabling virtual experiments to test and expand understanding of multicellular systems; shows applicability across cancer and developmental biology domains.
dataset: Multi-omics data from cancer and brain development tissues
metric: Model interpretability; alignment with experimental findings; predictive accuracy of emergent multicellular behavior
baseline: Existing mathematical modeling approaches; manual model implementation by specialists
result: Functional grammar enabling de novo and data-informed mechanistic model development with demonstrated predictions across cancer and developmental systems
enables: Enables democratization of virtual cell laboratory development by allowing biologists without extensive mathematical training to create, test, and refine computational models of multicellular behavior.
limits: Limited to systems where multi-omics data are available; grammar expressiveness bounded by natural language interpretation; scalability to genome-scale models unclear; validation still requires experimental confirmation.
open_questions: How well do grammar-generated models generalize to unseen biological contexts, and can the approach scale to whole-organism modeling of complex tissues?

problem: Complete description of cell state requires integration of size, shape, components, intracellular reactions, and environmental interactions across time; theoretical models and simulations must interpret enormous amounts of experimental data.
method: Review of challenges and progress in obtaining information for dynamical whole-cell models (DWCMs) of well-studied bacterial systems, discussing data requirements and integration approaches.
finding: Progress is being made on multiple fronts for obtaining necessary information to build dynamical whole-cell models, though challenges remain in integrating diverse datasets for complete cellular description.
dataset: Data from simple, well-studied bacterial systems (focus on E. coli)
metric: Completeness of cellular state description; model fidelity to experimental observations
baseline: Traditional experimental approaches; partial cellular models
result: Roadmap for data requirements and integration strategies for whole-cell model development
enables: Enables development of comprehensive computational models to predict cellular behavior, design experiments, and understand system-level cellular properties.
limits: Review focuses on well-studied bacterial systems; challenges are even greater for eukaryotic cells with complex compartmentalization; data integration remains technically difficult and labor-intensive.
open_questions: What is the minimal sufficient dataset needed to build predictive whole-cell models, and how can integration of data across different scales and experimental methods be systematized?

problem: Correction to published article on biophysical modeling of C. elegans neurons.
method: Correction notice for previous publication on single ion currents and whole-cell dynamics of C. elegans neuron types AWCon and RMD.
finding: Correction to DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0218738; specific corrections not detailed in this notice.
dataset: C. elegans neurons (AWCon and RMD)
metric: Biophysical model accuracy; ion current magnitudes
baseline: Original published model
result: Corrected biophysical model parameters
enables: Enables accurate biophysical modeling of C. elegans neural circuits for understanding neuronal dynamics and circuit function.
limits: Correction notice does not specify which results were affected or magnitude of corrections; original limitations of single-cell model remain.
open_questions: What aspects of the original model required correction, and how do corrections affect conclusions about AWCon and RMD neuron function?

problem: Understanding E. coli behavior and predicting its properties requires the most detailed computational model integrating knowledge of cell structure, genetics, and physiology.
method: Development of whole-cell computational model of E. coli integrating diverse experimental data; iterative framework for model improvement; discussion of approach and current version status.
finding: The E. coli Whole-Cell Modeling Project has developed and continues to refine detailed computational models of the E. coli cell incorporating current knowledge of cellular processes.
dataset: E. coli K-12 (model organism)
metric: Model comprehensiveness; prediction accuracy for cellular phenotypes
baseline: Previous partial cellular models; pathway-specific models
result: Most detailed computational model of E. coli integrating structure, genetics, and physiology
enables: Enables prediction of E. coli behavior under diverse conditions; accelerates systems biology research; provides framework for understanding cell biology across organisms.
limits: Computational complexity limits real-time prediction; model accuracy bounded by incomplete knowledge of all cellular interactions; difficult to update as new experimental data emerge.
open_questions: How complete must a whole-cell model be to make accurate predictions, and can the modeling framework be successfully applied to more complex organisms like yeast or human cells?

problem: Understanding minimal cell structure and function requires detailed whole-cell computational models that integrate stochastic and deterministic processes.
method: Development of hybrid stochastic/deterministic whole-cell model of JCVI-syn3A (minimal synthetic cell) incorporating both stochastic and deterministic simulation approaches.
finding: Hybrid stochastic/deterministic computational model of minimal cell JCVI-syn3A developed demonstrating integration of multiple simulation paradigms.
dataset: JCVI-syn3A minimal synthetic cell
metric: Model fidelity to minimal cell phenotypes; prediction accuracy for cellular dynamics
baseline: Previous deterministic-only or stochastic-only models
result: Functional hybrid whole-cell model of JCVI-syn3A
enables: Enables understanding of minimal essential cellular functions and how they integrate; provides simplified system for testing whole-cell modeling approaches.
limits: Minimal cell lacks complexity of natural cells; model validation limited by availability of experimental perturbation data; computational requirements for stochastic simulation limit scale.
open_questions: How do insights from minimal cell models transfer to understanding full natural cells, and what is the minimum genome/proteome necessary for cellular viability?

problem: Understanding how spatial organization and compartmentalization affect RNA splicing efficiency in mammalian cells requires spatially-resolved whole-cell models.
method: Constructed spatially-resolved whole HeLa cell model from experimental data incorporating nuclear compartments, organelles, and reaction networks for RNA splicing; performed stochastic simulations up to 15 minutes biological time.
finding: Spatial organization critically affects splicing efficiency: increased splicing particle localization in nuclear speckles disproportionately enhances mRNA splicing and reduces transcript noise; distance between genes and speckles significantly affects mRNA production rates.
dataset: HeLa mammalian cell (nucleus, cytoplasm, organelles)
metric: Assembled splicing particle yield; mRNA splicing efficiency; transcript noise; mRNA production rate
baseline: Non-spatial or simplified spatial models; empirical splicing data
result: Spatial model predicts 3-5x enhancement in splicing from speckle localization; demonstrates importance of genome-speckle organization
enables: Enables understanding of how nuclear organization regulates gene expression; provides framework for studying other spatially-dependent cellular processes.
limits: Model complexity requires significant computational resources; limited to 15 minutes simulation; cannot capture long-term cellular dynamics; HeLa cell-specific; simplifications of 3D organelle geometry.
open_questions: How do these spatial effects on splicing extend to other cell types and tissues, and what mechanisms dynamically regulate splicing particle localization in response to cellular signals?

problem: Whole-cell dynamical models of human cells are needed to understand cell biology and treat disease, but face significant challenges in data integration and model development.
method: Analyzed existing single pathway models; surveyed biomodeling community; synthesized experience from bacterial whole-cell models to propose Human Whole-Cell Modeling Project roadmap including technology and standards development.
finding: Human whole-cell models are becoming feasible; proposes concrete plan including technology development, standards development, and interdisciplinary collaboration as key pillars for success.
dataset: Human cells (framework applicable to multiple cell types)
metric: Model comprehensiveness; prediction accuracy for human cell behavior
baseline: Existing pathway-specific models; previous bacterial whole-cell modeling experience
result: Comprehensive research plan (Human Whole-Cell Modeling Project) for achieving human whole-cell models
enables: Enables development of mechanistic human cell models for understanding disease and designing therapies; provides roadmap for international collaboration.
limits: Plan describes future work rather than completed models; significant technical and resource challenges remain; human cell complexity far exceeds bacterial systems; data integration remains challenging.
open_questions: Which biological processes are essential to model first in human cells, and what are the minimal experimental datasets required to build predictive models?

problem: Interpreting in vitro concentration-viability data requires understanding actual cell-exposed concentrations rather than nominal applied concentrations; need models linking external dose to internal effects.
method: Developed Virtual Cell Based Assay (VCBA), a process-based integrated model combining: fate/transport model, cell partitioning model, cell growth/division model, toxicity/effects model, and experimental setup to simulate chemical kinetics and dynamics in cell-based assays.
finding: VCBA successfully simulates medium and intracellular chemical concentrations enabling design and interpretation of in vitro experiments; provides framework for in vitro to in vivo extrapolation using physiologically-based kinetic (PBK) models.
dataset: Cell-based in vitro assay systems
metric: Internal cell concentration; cell viability; toxicity endpoints
baseline: Nominal applied concentrations; non-integrated models
result: Functional VCBA model for multiple cell lines linking external dose to internal concentration and cellular effects
enables: Enables prediction of in vivo toxic doses from in vitro data and supports replacement of animal testing through improved in vitro-in vivo extrapolation.
limits: Model requires extensive physicochemical parameters and cell-specific properties; validation limited to tested cell lines and chemicals; simplifies intracellular compartmentalization.
open_questions: How well does VCBA performance generalize to diverse chemicals with different physicochemical properties, and can the approach scale to predict effects on multiple endpoints simultaneously?

problem: Replacing animal testing in toxicity testing requires predicting in vivo toxic doses from in vitro concentration-response data; need methods to extrapolate across experimental systems.
method: Virtual Cell Based Assay (VCBA) extended to multiple cell lines (BALB/c 3T3, HepG2, HepaRG, A549, cardiomyocytes) using ordinary differential equations and physicochemical parameters to predict time-dependent chemical concentrations.
finding: VCBA enables design and refinement of in vitro experiments and extrapolation of in vitro effective concentrations to in vivo doses for risk assessment; applications demonstrated in HTS experiment design, hazard identification, and risk assessment.
dataset: Multiple mammalian cell lines (3T3, hepatocytes, lung, cardiomyocytes)
metric: Internal cell concentration; EC50/IC50 values; toxicity classification; risk quotient
baseline: Animal testing; traditional in vitro approaches without extrapolation
result: VCBA applications to HTS design, hazard identification, and in vivo dose prediction for multiple cell lines
enables: Enables replacement of animal testing through improved in vitro predictions; supports chemical risk assessment using only in vitro and in silico tools; facilitates HTS experimental design.
limits: Limited to cell lines tested (primary cell systems not developed); extrapolation accuracy depends on model calibration; cannot capture tissue-level effects or systemic metabolism.
open_questions: Can VCBA be successfully applied to nanomaterials and complex mixtures, and how can the approach incorporate metabolic activation relevant to in vivo hepatic processing?

problem: Building virtual cells requires understanding protein network dynamics; current approaches lack comprehensive proteome-scale perturbation data and models to predict complex cellular responses.
method: Generated 38 million perturbed protein measurements in breast cancer cell lines; developed neural ordinary differential equation foundation model (ProteinTalks) for learning cellular protein network dynamics during pretraining.
finding: ProteinTalks foundation model encodes protein networks with improved predictive accuracy across downstream tasks including drug efficacy/synergy prediction, resistance protein identification, and integration with clinical patient data for prognosis prediction.
dataset: Breast cancer cell lines (large-scale perturbation proteomics); patient-derived tumor xenografts; clinical patient data
metric: Drug efficacy prediction accuracy; drug synergy prediction; prognosis prediction for cancer patients
baseline: Previous pathway-based models; non-proteome models; standard machine learning approaches
result: Foundation model predicting drug efficacy, synergy, and identifying resistance-associated proteins; prognosis prediction improved with clinical integration
enables: Enables discovery of novel drug combinations; personalized drug response prediction; identification of resistance mechanisms; integration of proteomics with patient outcomes for precision medicine.
limits: Limited to breast cancer cell lines; foundation model trained on specific proteome dataset; generalization to other cancer types unclear; interpretation of learned representations challenging; limited to tested cell lines.
open_questions: How well does ProteinTalks transfer to other cancer types and normal tissues, can it predict responses to combination therapies with >2 drugs, and what is the mechanism of predicted resistance-associated proteins?

problem: Predicting chemical toxicity in 3D spheroid cell cultures requires extending 2D models to account for diffusion gradients and spatial heterogeneity in three-dimensional systems.
method: Extended 2D Virtual Cell Based Assay (VCBA) to 3D spheroid model using quasi-diffusion approximation with cells arranged in concentric layers; modeled chemical diffusion and toxicity response in 3D geometry.
finding: 3D VCBA model successfully predicts acetaminophen (APAP) toxicity in HepaRG spheroids showing close correlation between simulated and empirical chemical concentrations and viability outcomes.
dataset: HepaRG hepatocyte 3D spheroid cultures
metric: Internal chemical concentration in 3D spheroid; cell viability in multiple layers; toxicity prediction accuracy
baseline: 2D VCBA model; non-spatial spheroid models; empirical 3D culture measurements
result: 3D VCBA model successfully predicts chemical toxicity in spheroid cultures with good agreement to experimental measurements
enables: Enables improved prediction of chemical toxicity using 3D culture systems more representative of in vivo tissue; supports refinement of in vitro-in vivo extrapolation.
limits: Limited to spheroid geometry; single chemical tested (acetaminophen); assumes homogeneous cell distribution; computational cost limits practical application; requires extensive calibration.
open_questions: Can the 3D model extend to non-spheroidal tissue geometries and heterogeneous cell distributions, and does it improve in vivo prediction accuracy compared to 2D models?

problem: Virtual cell culture training must provide accessible, realistic simulation of mammalian cell culture techniques while allowing interactive decision-making that recapitulates in-person laboratory experience.
method: Designed immersive 3D browser-based virtual mammalian cell culture simulation with interactive decision-making and technical feedback; assessed through qualitative study with undergraduate and graduate students in biotechnology courses.
finding: 73% of students found virtual simulation had clear real-world connections; 57% agreed it aided understanding of cell culture; >50% by course end agreed simulation was realistic and required critical thinking.
dataset: Student cohorts (n=87 fall, n=96 spring for post-lab; n=73 fall, n=74 spring for end-of-course surveys)
metric: Student perception of real-world relevance; perceived aid in understanding; perceived realism and critical thinking demand
baseline: Traditional in-person cell culture laboratories
result: Virtual simulation perceived as realistic and pedagogically valuable by 50-73% of students; lowers accessibility barriers
enables: Enables accessible cell culture training for students lacking in-person lab access or at institutions with resource limitations; supplements traditional labs for skill development.
limits: Survey-based assessment; limited to two course iterations; student self-reported learning (not objectively measured); immersion level depends on computer/display technology; may not fully replace hands-on experience.
open_questions: Does the virtual simulation result in equivalent skill competency compared to traditional labs, and how does learning transfer to actual hands-on cell culture practice?

problem: Revisiting the blueprint for an interpretable virtual cell requires reassessing approaches, methods, and feasibility given recent advances and lessons learned in whole-cell modeling.
method: Review article revisiting previously proposed blueprints for whole-cell modeling with focus on interpretability and recent developments.
finding: Updates to whole-cell modeling blueprint incorporating recent advances and lessons learned; emphasis on interpretable models for broader research community application.
dataset: Various cellular systems reviewed
metric: Model interpretability; framework generalizability; community adoption
baseline: Previous whole-cell modeling blueprints and proposals
result: Updated comprehensive framework for building interpretable virtual cells
enables: Enables broader adoption of whole-cell modeling by emphasizing interpretability over pure prediction accuracy; facilitates knowledge discovery alongside forecasting.
limits: Review-based; full article content not available in abstract; tradeoffs between interpretability and predictive power unclear from abstract alone.
open_questions: What design choices most impact model interpretability, and how can interpretable models maintain predictive accuracy comparable to black-box approaches?

problem: Understanding cellular structures and processes at high detail requires interactive visualization and simulation combining multiple modeling paradigms in realistic 3D environments.
method: Developed Eukaryo, a 3D interactive eukaryotic cell simulation implemented in game engine combining agent-based and mathematical modeling via visual scripting; demonstrates gene expression, energy production, and waste removal.
finding: Eukaryo successfully demonstrates cellular architecture, components, and macromolecular dynamics with high fidelity and real-time interactivity using virtual reality; superior representation compared to traditional media.
dataset: Generic eukaryotic cell with processes (gene expression, metabolism, waste removal)
metric: Model fidelity to cellular structure; visual clarity; interactivity response time
baseline: Illustrations, videos, and traditional cell simulations
result: Functional 3D interactive eukaryotic cell simulation with high visual fidelity and VR integration
enables: Enables immersive learning and exploration of cellular structures and dynamics; provides interactive platform for hypothesis testing about cellular processes.
limits: Limited to generic eukaryotic cell; computational cost limits scale to whole-cell detail; VR display access limits usage; validation of biological accuracy unclear.
open_questions: How does user learning from immersive 3D simulation compare to traditional pedagogy, and can the approach scale to represent genome-scale networks?

problem: Understanding amygdala circuitry and its role in pain requires integrating cell-type specific physiological data and evaluating emergent system-level neural responses.
method: Developed agent-based computational model of two inhibitory amygdala neuron populations (PKC delta and somatostatin) using neural network connectivity and cell-type-specific physiological parameters estimated from literature and new experimental data.
finding: Model demonstrates relative proportion of PKC delta and somatostatin neurons is key parameter predicting pain; produces pain changes consistent with published studies; differences with behavioral data suggest new wet-lab experiments.
dataset: Central amygdala tissue; two inhibitory neuron types (PKC delta and somatostatin expressing)
metric: Pain score (cumulative pro-nociceptive and anti-nociceptive activity); neuron population proportion effects
baseline: Published physiological data; previous behavioral pain studies
result: Agent-based model predicting pain dynamics for three different neuron proportion scenarios with consistency to experimental literature
enables: Enables hypothesis generation for wet-lab investigation of amygdala pain circuits; provides framework for understanding how cell-type composition affects system function.
limits: Model limited to two inhibitory neuron types; excludes excitatory neurons and other circuit elements; pain is modeled abstractly; parameter uncertainty from literature data; limited experimental validation.
open_questions: How do excitatory neurons and other cell types modulate pain responses, what experimental manipulations should be performed to validate model predictions, and how does the amygdala interact with descending pain inhibition pathways?

problem: Predicting vascular mechanics and smooth muscle cell behavior requires coupling macroscopic finite element models of tissue mechanics with microscale agent-based models of cellular dynamics.
method: Developed coupling approach between finite element model of coronary artery mechanics and microscale agent-based model of smooth muscle cells using trilinear interpolation to connect model scales.
finding: Successfully coupled finite element tissue-level mechanics model with agent-based single-cell model enabling multiscale prediction of vascular responses to mechanical loading.
dataset: Coronary artery tissue with smooth muscle cells
metric: Tissue-level stress/strain; cell-level migration and proliferation; coupling fidelity
baseline: Single-scale finite element models or single-cell agent-based models alone
result: Functional multiscale model coupling macroscopic and microscopic vascular dynamics
enables: Enables prediction of how tissue-level mechanical forces influence cell-level responses and vice versa; facilitates understanding of mechanotransduction in vasculature.
limits: Computational cost of multiscale coupling limits resolution and temporal scale; interpolation-based coupling introduces error; limited to specific vascular system; full results not available in abstract.
open_questions: How do multiscale predictions improve over single-scale models in accuracy, what is the minimum smooth muscle cell representation necessary, and how does approach scale to complex vessel geometries?

problem: Understanding collective cell migration and jamming transitions in epithelial sheets requires computational models that capture cell-cell interactions and tissue-level dynamics.
method: Developed agent-based model to investigate chain-migration-mediated jamming-unjamming transitions in corneal epithelial cell sheets.
finding: Agent-based model successfully captures jamming-unjamming dynamics in corneal epithelial cell migration; identifies chain migration as key mechanism regulating collective cell motion.
dataset: Corneal epithelial cell sheets
metric: Cell migration velocity; jamming fraction; migration pattern topology
baseline: Non-spatial models; models ignoring chain migration
result: Agent-based model demonstrating chain migration-mediated jamming transitions in epithelial sheets
enables: Enables understanding of wound healing dynamics and collective cell migration in epithelial tissues; identifies therapeutic targets for modulating migration.
limits: Limited to corneal epithelium; assumes 2D sheet geometry; simplified cell properties; full results not available in abstract.
open_questions: How do model predictions compare to quantitative experimental observations of corneal cell migration, and do the jamming dynamics extend to other epithelial tissue types?

problem: Preclinical immunotherapy studies take months to years and require extensive resources; bottom-up agent-based models can accelerate computational exploration of immune-tumor interactions.
method: Developed ACESO, a 3D agent-based simulation modeling interactions between immune and tumor cells; uses particle-based agents to represent individual cells and their interactions.
finding: ACESO successfully demonstrates 3D agent-based approach for modeling immune-tumor cell interactions at cellular level, capturing dynamics not possible with population-level ordinary/partial differential equation models.
dataset: Generic immune and tumor cells
metric: Tumor growth dynamics; immune infiltration; immune-tumor contact frequency
baseline: ODE/PDE population models; wet-lab preclinical studies
result: Functional 3D agent-based immune-tumor simulation showing cellular-level interaction dynamics
enables: Enables rapid computational exploration of immunotherapy strategies before wet-lab studies; allows testing of many experimental configurations in silico.
limits: Generic cell representation; limited validation against experimental immunotherapy; computational cost limits simulation scale and duration; cell heterogeneity not fully captured.
open_questions: How well do ACESO predictions match experimental immunotherapy outcomes, can the model incorporate checkpoint inhibitors and other therapeutic interventions, and what cell heterogeneity is essential to model?

problem: Predicting pattern formation in pluripotent stem cell populations requires understanding whether high-level behavioral rules are sufficient or if detailed biological modeling is necessary.
method: Applied agent-based modeling to study pattern formation in pluripotent stem cell populations; tested five models with four biologically plausible motility rules: velocity, directional persistence time, directional movements, and border effects.
finding: Pattern formation in pluripotent stem cells is governed by complex combination of rules; cell directional movements based on local density play important role in pattern formation; predictive power sufficient at behavioral level without deep biological detail.
dataset: Pluripotent stem cell cultures (aggregate populations)
metric: Pattern formation fidelity; model prediction accuracy; variability explanation
baseline: Empirical pattern formation observations; deterministic models
result: Agent-based model explaining pluripotent stem cell pattern formation through behavioral rules; predicts much of observed variability
enables: Enables prediction and control of stem cell population organization for therapeutic applications; suggests minimal biological detail needed for pattern prediction.
limits: Limited to 2D simulations; cell phenotype held constant; no molecular mechanisms modeled; training and validation on same dataset limits generalization.
open_questions: Do these behavioral rules generalize to 3D tissue structures and other stem cell types, what molecular mechanisms underlie directional movements based on local density, and can the model predict response to external stimuli?

problem: Whole-cell model output is complex and high-dimensional making interpretation challenging; need methods to extract biological insights from metabolic flux predictions.
method: Developed analysis pipeline combining machine learning, dimensionality reduction, and network analysis to interpret metabolic reaction fluxes from Mycoplasma genitalium whole-cell model single gene knockout simulations.
finding: Identified metabolic reaction behaviors that correlate with phenotypic classes of gene knockouts; reaction markers identified that indicate phenotypic class; pipeline enables understanding of complex whole-cell model output without detailed knowledge of constituent parts.
dataset: Mycoplasma genitalium whole-cell model predictions for single gene knockouts
metric: Phenotypic class prediction accuracy; reaction marker consistency; network analysis metrics
baseline: Direct inspection of metabolic fluxes; non-systematic interpretation
result: Functional pipeline for metabolic flux interpretation identifying cellular subsystems that malfunction after knockouts and predictive reaction markers
enables: Enables extraction of biological insights from complex whole-cell models; facilitates understanding of gene function without detailed mechanistic knowledge; supports genome engineering design.
limits: Limited to metabolic aspects of whole-cell model; machine learning requires training data; generalization to other organisms unclear; dimensionality reduction may lose biologically relevant information.
open_questions: Can the pipeline identify causal metabolic dependencies, does it extend to non-metabolic aspects of whole-cell models, and how does performance scale to larger organisms with more complex metabolic networks?
